title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a man called Omar Malik, who makes costumes for theatre productions.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today we're speaking to Omar Malik, who makes costumes for theatre productions. Omar, how did you get into this work? || Guest: My aunt was a tailor, and I learnt basic sewing from her, but I didn't think of theatre then. [20]I became interested when a school play needed costumes quickly and I enjoyed making clothes that helped tell the story[/20]. || Interviewer: What do you do when you start a new production? || Guest: I read the play first, of course. Then [21]I meet the director to understand the world they want the audience to see[/21]. After that I draw ideas and look for suitable materials. || Interviewer: What is the hardest part of the job? || Guest: People might think it's sewing tiny details, but I enjoy that. [22]The most difficult part is changing a costume quickly when an actor finds it uncomfortable during rehearsals[/22]. You have to solve the problem fast. || Interviewer: Do actors usually have strong opinions about their costumes? || Guest: Yes, and that's helpful most of the time. [23]If an actor feels the costume is wrong, they often move differently, so I try to listen carefully[/23]. A small change can make a big difference. || Interviewer: What costume are you especially proud of? || Guest: Last year I made a coat for a comedy character. It looked simple at first, but [24]it had hidden pockets and pieces that fell off at exactly the right moments[/24]. The audience laughed every night. || Interviewer: What advice would you give someone who wants this job? || Guest: Learn to sew well, but don't stop there. [25]You also need to understand people, because you'll work with actors, directors and stage managers under pressure[/25]. Being calm matters. || Interviewer: Has your job changed with new technology? || Guest: A little. We use computer drawings more than before, and online shops make it easier to find unusual materials. But theatre is still very practical. You still need pins, scissors, hands and patience. A costume has to survive bright lights, quick changes and sometimes dancing. || Interviewer: Do you watch every show you work on? || Guest: I watch the first night if I can, because that's when I know whether everything works. After that I may only go back if something needs repair. It is strange to see the costumes on stage. They stop feeling like mine and become part of the actors' characters. || Interviewer: What happens to costumes after a production finishes? || Guest: Some are stored and used again, especially coats or hats that can fit different stories. Others are taken apart so the fabric can be reused. We try not to waste materials because theatre budgets are often small. || Interviewer: Do you prefer historical or modern costumes? || Guest: Historical costumes are beautiful, but modern ones can be harder than people think. If a character wears jeans and a jacket, the audience immediately knows whether it looks right. Everyday clothes have to be chosen very carefully. || Interviewer: Do audiences notice your work? || Guest: Usually only if something looks wrong, and that is fine. A good costume should support the story, not shout for attention all the time. I feel successful when people believe the character completely without thinking too much about the clothes.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today we're speaking to Omar Malik, who makes costumes for theatre productions. Omar, how did you get into this work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: My aunt was a tailor, and I learnt basic sewing from her, but I didn't think of theatre then. I became interested when a school play needed costumes quickly and I enjoyed making clothes that helped tell the story.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you do when you start a new production?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I read the play first, of course. Then I meet the director to understand the world they want the audience to see. After that I draw ideas and look for suitable materials.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What is the hardest part of the job?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: People might think it's sewing tiny details, but I enjoy that. The most difficult part is changing a costume quickly when an actor finds it uncomfortable during rehearsals. You have to solve the problem fast.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do actors usually have strong opinions about their costumes?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, and that's helpful most of the time. If an actor feels the costume is wrong, they often move differently, so I try to listen carefully. A small change can make a big difference.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What costume are you especially proud of?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Last year I made a coat for a comedy character. It looked simple at first, but it had hidden pockets and pieces that fell off at exactly the right moments. The audience laughed every night.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What advice would you give someone who wants this job?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Learn to sew well, but don't stop there. You also need to understand people, because you'll work with actors, directors and stage managers under pressure. Being calm matters.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Has your job changed with new technology?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: A little. We use computer drawings more than before, and online shops make it easier to find unusual materials. But theatre is still very practical. You still need pins, scissors, hands and patience. A costume has to survive bright lights, quick changes and sometimes dancing.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you watch every show you work on?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I watch the first night if I can, because that's when I know whether everything works. After that I may only go back if something needs repair. It is strange to see the costumes on stage. They stop feeling like mine and become part of the actors' characters.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What happens to costumes after a production finishes?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Some are stored and used again, especially coats or hats that can fit different stories. Others are taken apart so the fabric can be reused. We try not to waste materials because theatre budgets are often small.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you prefer historical or modern costumes?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Historical costumes are beautiful, but modern ones can be harder than people think. If a character wears jeans and a jacket, the audience immediately knows whether it looks right. Everyday clothes have to be chosen very carefully.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do audiences notice your work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Usually only if something looks wrong, and that is fine. A good costume should support the story, not shout for attention all the time. I feel successful when people believe the character completely without thinking too much about the clothes.

questions.question: How did Omar become interested in theatre costumes?

options.A: His aunt invited him to work in a theatre.
options.B: He studied theatre design at college.
options.C: He helped make costumes for a school play.

questions.explanation: Omar says he became interested when a school play needed costumes quickly.

questions.question: What does Omar do after reading a new play?
questions.explanation: He says he meets the director to understand the world they want the audience to see.

questions.question: What does Omar find most difficult?
questions.explanation: He says the most difficult part is changing a costume quickly when an actor finds it uncomfortable.

questions.question: Why does Omar listen to actors’ opinions?

options.A: They usually know more about clothes than he does.
options.B: They decide how much the costumes cost.
options.C: The wrong costume can affect how they perform.

questions.explanation: He says an actor may move differently if the costume feels wrong.

questions.question: Why was Omar proud of the comedy coat?

options.B: It was made from very expensive material.
options.C: It was copied for another production.

questions.explanation: He says it had hidden pockets and pieces that fell off at the right moments.

questions.question: What advice does Omar give to future costume makers?

options.A: Choose one theatre to work with.

questions.explanation: He says costume makers need to understand people and stay calm under pressure.
